questions.question: Summary of His First Flight (Liam O'Flaherty)
questions.answer:
The story "His First Flight" is about a young seagull who is terrified of flying. His two brothers and his little sister have already flown away from the ledge where the family lived on a high cliff by the sea, but the young seagull cannot bring himself to take the plunge. He feels certain that his wings will not support him.

His parents try everything to make him fly — calling, scolding, and even threatening to let him starve on the ledge. But he simply cannot move. Twenty-four hours pass and nobody comes near him. He is left alone, hungry and desperate.

Finally, his mother flies towards him with a piece of fish in her beak. Maddened by hunger, the young seagull dives at the fish and falls off the ledge into the air. A moment of terror seizes him, but then he feels his wings spread outwards and the wind rushing against his feathers. He is flying! He soars, dives, and screams with joy.

His whole family flies around him, praising and encouraging him. He lands on the sea, floats on it, and his family offers him scraps of food. He has made his first flight. The story teaches us that sometimes fear can only be overcome when we are pushed to act, and that courage often comes in the moment of necessity.

questions.question: Part 1: The Young Seagull's Fear (Lines 1-15)
questions.answer: The young seagull is alone on his ledge. His two brothers and sister had already flown away the day before. He had been afraid to fly — when he ran to the brink and tried to flap his wings, the vast expanse of sea far below terrified him. He felt his wings would never support him. Even though his siblings, whose wings were shorter than his, had managed to fly, he could not muster the courage. His parents scolded him, called shrilly, and threatened to let him starve unless he flew. But he could not move.

questions.question: Part 2: Alone and Starving (Lines 16-40)
questions.answer: Twenty-four hours pass and nobody comes near the young seagull. He watches his family from the ledge — his brothers, sister, and parents flying about, perfecting the art of flight, catching fish, and walking on the plateau. He is desperately hungry, having found only a dried piece of mackerel tail and even gnawed at dried eggshell. He walks back and forth on the ledge trying to find a way to reach his parents without flying, but the ledge ends in sheer precipices on each side. He tries to catch his parents' attention by standing on one leg and pretending to sleep, but they ignore him.

questions.question: Part 3: The Mother's Trick (Lines 41-60)
questions.answer: Only the young seagull's mother is looking at him. She is standing on a high hump, tearing at a piece of fish. The sight of the food maddens the hungry seagull. He cries out 'Ga, ga, ga' begging for food. His mother screams back mockingly. But he keeps calling plaintively, and after a minute she picks up a piece of fish and flies across to him with it. He leans out eagerly, tapping the rock with his feet. But when she is just opposite him, abreast of the ledge, she halts — wings motionless, the fish almost within reach of his beak. He waits, and then, maddened by hunger, he dives at the fish and falls outwards and downwards into space.

questions.question: Part 4: The First Flight (Lines 61-end)
questions.answer: A monstrous terror seizes the young seagull and his heart stands still. But then he feels his wings spread outwards, the wind rushing against his breast feathers and under his stomach. He is not falling — he is soaring gradually downwards and outwards. He flaps his wings and soars upward, uttering a joyous scream. His mother swoops past him, his father flies over screaming, and his brothers and sister fly around him, soaring and diving. He completely forgets his fear and commences to dive and soar, shrieking shrilly. He flies over the ocean, sees the vast green sea with little ridges beneath him. His family lands on the sea and beckons him. He drops his legs to stand on the water, his legs sink in, and he floats. His family screams, praises him, and offers him scraps of food. He has made his first flight.

questions.question: About the Author
questions.answer:
**Liam O'Flaherty (1896-1984)**

Liam O'Flaherty was an Irish novelist and short story writer. He also played a significant role in the Irish literary renaissance. Two constant themes in his works are nature and Ireland. The best of his short stories are in Irish and were widely acclaimed after they were translated into English.

questions.question: Theme of the Story
questions.answer:
The central theme of the story is **overcoming fear**. The young seagull's fear of flying represents any fear that holds us back from achieving what we are capable of. The story shows that:

1. **Fear can paralyse us** — even though the seagull's wings were longer than his siblings', fear prevented him from using them.
2. **Sometimes we need a push** — the mother's trick of tempting him with food was the push he needed.
3. **Hunger (necessity) can overcome fear** — desperation forced the seagull to act.
4. **Once we take the first step, confidence follows** — the moment the seagull started flying, he forgot all his fears.
5. **Family support matters** — his family encouraged and praised him once he flew.
6. **We often underestimate our own abilities** — the seagull's wings could support him all along; only his fear told him otherwise.
